2. Ring Theory

2.1. Rings

The integers don’t form a field since division isn’t a closed operation, i.e. multiplicative inverse does not exist. Let’s define a new structure that is almost a field but excludes this requirement.

Definition 6. A commutative ring is a set equipped with addition and multiplication such that

  1. is an abelian group.
  2. is commutative and associative with multiplicative identity
  3. Multiplication distributes over additoin

Examples of rings:

  • Any field .
  • and
  • The polynomial ring .
  • The zero ring,
  • Product ring (addition and multiplication are defined componentwise).
Definition 7. A ring homomorphism preserves addition, multiplication, and the multiplicative identity. Preserving additive identity is implied.

The third condition must be specified since otherwise we are not guaranteed for to hold. Consider the zero map which sends all elements to zero in which is not a homomorphism unless

Examples

  • 3 is an injective ring homomorphism embedding in .
  • The image is a subring of .
  • with is not a ring homomorphism since . However, with is a ring homomorphism.
  • The evaluation map , with is a ring homomorphism.
Definition 8. is a unit if there exists such that . Let denote the set of units.

Lemma 7.

  1. if and only if is a unit.
  2. The subset of units forms a group under multiplication.
Proof

Suppose . Then there exists such that , implying is a unit. Suppose is unit. Then for all . Thus .

□

2.2. Ideals

We defined normal subgroups in group theory based on the nice properties of kernels of group homomorphisms. The kernel of a ring homomorphism is (1) an additive group and (2) closed under left multiplication by elements of .

Definition 9. A subset is an ideal if it is an additive group closed under multiplication by any element in .

The union of ideals is not necessarily an ideal since closure under addition may not hold. For example, consider and as ideals of . does not belong to However, we have something weaker for a nested ordered family of ideals.

Lemma 8.

  1. If are ideals, then so are the sum of ideals and the intersection of ideals (but not necessarily ).
  2. If is a nested ordered family of ideals or a ring, then their union is an ideal.
  3. The preimage of an ideal is an ideal. The image of an ideal is not necessarily an ideal.

For (3), consider . The image is not an ideal in . More generally, If is surjective, then is an ideal.

Example: and (Bezout’s identity in 18.701)

Denote . Note that any ideal containing and contains . Thus we call the minimal ideal generated by the generating set .

Definition 10.

  1. An ideal is a principal ideal if there exists a generator such that .
  2. A ring is a principal ideal ring (PIR) if all its ideals are principal. A PIR that is an integral domain is a PID.

Example:

  1. generated by any unit.
  2. is a principal ideal in .
  3. is not principle in .
  4. is not principal. This is formaliized through the notion of irreducibility later.
  5. When is not prime, is a PIR but not a PID.
  6. is an example of a PIR that is not a PID.

** commutative diagram **

For example: (consider the evaluation map )

Suppose is principal, and consider the quotient ring . We can think of as the ring obtained by imposing the relation , or “killing” . Two elements are in the same coset if and . Clearly, setting enforces in . It also works the other way around. This is known as adding relations.

The Correspondence theorem asserts that killing in and then killing in yields , so the order in which we add relations does not matter.

Example 2. Show that the quotient ring is isomorphic to .

Solution. Recall that is obtained by killing in , and the preimage of is . Thus . We may introduce the relations in the opposite order, first killing . This yields , and the image of is . Thus taking yields .

2.4. Adjoining Elements

The first isomorphism theorem implies . Note that we constructed a new ring including a base ring as a subring and a new element . Similar thing with generating from . This is called a ring extension.

For example,

  • .

This is used later to show that is a UFD implies is a UFD.

Localization generalizes this construction to any commutative ring, not just integral domains. If we have a multiplicatively closed set , then the localization is a new ring whose elements are fractions with numerators in and denominators in .

2.6. Maximal Ideals

Definition 13. A proper ideal is maximal satisfying the following condition: if is another ideal with , then either or .

Proposition 10.

  1. is maximal if and only if implies .
  2. A maximal ideal is prime.
  3. is maximal if and only if is a field.
Proof

  1. Follows from the definition and the fact that the sum of two ideals is an ideal.

  2. Suppose and . Then from (1). Then , implying that . Thus is prime.

  3. Forward proof. Suppose . Then by (1) so there exists such that , implying that . Thus every nonzero element in has a multiplicative inverse. The rest of the field definitions are satisfied since is a ring.

    Backward proof. Suppose is a field. Consider . Then there exists a multiplicative inverse such that . This implies , i.e. some , implying that . Thus . Invoke (1).

□

Examples

  • In , prime ideals are also maximal.
  • is maximal, since is a field.
  • is not maximal since is not a field.
Proposition 11 (Prime ⟺ Maximal in a PID). If is a PID, then nonzero prime ideals are also maximal. I.e. is prime if and only if only is maximal.
Proof

Suppose is prime, and . Then must satisfy divides . But in PIDs, prime elements are irreducible, so we must have , implying that is maximal.

This proof doesn’t work for the zero ring.

□

To summarize, in a PID the following statements are equivalent:

  1. is prime.
  2. is irreducibility.
  3. is maximal.

Lemma 9 (Sufficient conditions for a ring to be a field).

  1. An integral domain with finite cardinality is a field.
  2. A ring is a field if and only if it contains no proper nontrivial ideals. That is, it has two ideals, the zero ideal and the unit ideal ( itself).
Proof


  1. Look at left multiplication and argue each element has a multiplicative inverse.
  2. () Let be a nontrivial ideal, and let . Then , so is the unit ideal.
    It suffices to show that all nonzero elements in are units. For all , by hypothesis we have , thus is a unit.

□

Corollary. Let be a field. Then every homomorphism to a nonzero ring is injective. , so the kernel does not include . Thus it must be the zero ideal.
